text: The surgeon points out the pink stomach tissue, pushing it up against the defect where the diaphragm has separated around the esophagus, allowing the stomach to slide into the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a heat dividing instrument to sever attachments between the diaphragm and stomach, focusing on freeing the esophagus from the diaphragm.

The surgeon divides attachments between the liver and stomach, carefully sparing a small nerve visible in the tissue bridge below. Once the tissue is fully freed, the defect will be closed, and the stomach wrap for the fundoplication will be initiated.
The camera view shows the area inside the chest cavity, with the pink esophagus visible below. The nerve remains intact just beneath the esophagus.
The surgeon divides the remaining tissue to gain additional length for the fundoplication wrap, lifting the esophagus to visualize the diaphragm encircling it below.

The surgeon closes the diaphragm below the esophagus, addressing the V-shaped defect. Non-absorbable sutures are placed through felt pledges to reinforce the diaphragm closure, following the creation of a tunnel below the stomach.

The surgeon continues the closure of the diaphragm using non-absorbable sutures and pledges, ensuring the stomach remains in place to prevent it from sliding into the chest, which could cause heartburn symptoms.

The surgeon places an additional suture to secure the closure below the diaphragm, adjacent to the esophagus. The sutures are positioned to ensure adequate space for swallowing while stabilizing the esophagus.

The surgeon frees the stomach from the left side near the spleen, visible on the right side of the screen. Purple lines representing blood vessels are divided using a harmonic scalpel, allowing the stomach to be maneuvered behind itself to form the wrap.
The surgeon brings the backside of the stomach around and positions it over a rubber stent, ensuring the wrap remains open and does not become too tight.
The surgeon sews the wrap together using additional pledges, completing the wrap over the stint. The stint is then removed, and the space is checked to ensure there is adequate room for swallowing.

The surgeon places two additional sutures around the wrap on the left side and into the diaphragm to prevent it from sliding into the chest. An additional suture is placed through the wrap to the diaphragm at the back, completing the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
